steps.prompt: Which word is the main clause verb?

hints.duceret: 'duceret' is subjunctive in the 'si' clause — not the main verb

hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'paream' — 'parerem' with -rem ending is imperfect subjunctive
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect indicative would be 'parebam' — 'parerem' is subjunctive
hints.Pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'paruissem' — 'parerem' is imperfect subjunctive

hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are the parts of 'parare' (to prepare) — a 1st conjugation verb
hints.patior, pati, passus sum: These are the parts of 'pati' (to suffer) — a deponent verb
hints.pario, parere, peperi, partum: These are the parts of 'parere' (to give birth) — a different verb with the same infinitive form

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative would be 'ducit' — 'duceret' is subjunctive
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect indicative would be 'ducebat' — 'duceret' has the subjunctive -ret ending
hints.Perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'duxerit' — 'duceret' is imperfect subjunctive

hints.doceo, docere, docui, doctum: These are the parts of 'docere' (to teach)
hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are the parts of 'dicere' (to say)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'dare' (to give)

steps.prompt: What type of conditional is 'si … duceret … parerem'?
steps.target: Present contrary-to-fact (imperfect subjunctive in both clauses)
choices: Present contrary-to-fact (imperfect subjunctive in both clauses)

hints.Simple/open condition: Simple conditions use the indicative — both verbs here are imperfect subjunctive
hints.Past contrary-to-fact: Past contrary-to-fact uses pluperfect subjunctive — this uses imperfect (present time)
hints.Future less vivid: Future less vivid uses present subjunctive — this uses imperfect subjunctive (present contrary-to-fact)

hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'mortis' — 'mors' is nominative, subject of 'duceret'

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'mors, mortis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'mors, mortis' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. in -us — 'mors, mortis' has gen. -is (3rd)

translation: If death were already leading you away, Catiline, I would willingly obey.
